sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A glucose tolerance test was performed in healthy participants and patients with impaired insulin secretion. After drinking a glucose solution, healthy participants showed a moderate rise in blood glucose followed by a rapid decline toward baseline as insulin rose. Patients with impaired insulin secretion showed a higher glucose peak and slower return toward baseline. Their insulin levels rose only slightly. The instructor emphasized that insulin promotes glucose uptake and storage after meals.
questions.stem: Why does blood glucose remain elevated longer in the impaired group?

choices.C: Insulin secretion is insufficient to promote normal glucose uptake and storage
choices.B: Glucose cannot be absorbed from the intestine

explanations.C: Correct. The impaired group has a weak insulin response, so glucose clearance from blood is reduced after the oral glucose load.
explanations.A: Absent glucagon would not explain post-meal hyperglycemia here.
explanations.B: Glucose clearly enters blood because levels rise.
explanations.D: Hemoglobin concentration is unrelated to the glucose tolerance curve.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Blood glucose and insulin responses after oral glucose intake in healthy and impaired insulin-secretion groups.

questions.stem: Which tissue response is directly promoted by insulin?

choices.B: Increased glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue via GLUT4 translocation
choices.C: Increased ketone production after a meal

explanations.B: Correct. Insulin promotes GLUT4 translocation in muscle and adipose tissue and encourages storage pathways such as glycogen synthesis.
explanations.C: Insulin generally suppresses ketogenesis in the fed state.
explanations.D: Insulin tends to support anabolic processes, not inhibit all protein synthesis.

questions.stem: Which curve in Figure 1 best identifies impaired insulin secretion?

choices.D: The insulin curve that rises only slightly after glucose intake
choices.C: The baseline glucose value at time 0 only

explanations.D: Correct. The impaired group shows a small insulin increase despite high glucose levels, directly indicating inadequate insulin secretion.
explanations.A: The healthy glucose curve returns toward baseline efficiently.
explanations.B: The healthy insulin curve rises robustly after glucose intake.
explanations.C: Baseline alone does not reveal the impaired response.

questions.stem: What would glucagon normally do during fasting?

choices.B: Promote GLUT4 translocation in adipose tissue
choices.C: Lower blood glucose by storing it in glycogen only
choices.D: Digest dietary triglycerides in the intestinal lumen

explanations.A: Correct. Glucagon helps maintain blood glucose during fasting by stimulating glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis in the liver.
explanations.B: Insulin, not glucagon, promotes GLUT4 translocation.
explanations.C: Glucagon raises rather than lowers blood glucose.
explanations.D: Digestive enzymes, not glucagon, digest triglycerides in the lumen.

questions.stem: Which statement best distinguishes endocrine from exocrine signaling?

choices.D: Endocrine hormones travel through blood to distant targets
choices.A: Endocrine signals are secreted only into ducts
choices.B: Exocrine secretions always act through nuclear receptors

explanations.D: Correct. Endocrine glands release hormones into the bloodstream, allowing action at distant target tissues.
explanations.A: Duct secretion is characteristic of exocrine glands.
explanations.B: Exocrine secretions do not always act through nuclear receptors.
explanations.C: Endocrine hormones require receptors for specificity.

questions.passage: A renal physiology experiment measured urine osmolarity and urine volume before and after administration of an ADH analog. After the analog, urine volume decreased while urine osmolarity increased. In a second condition, a collecting-duct aquaporin blocker prevented most of the ADH analog effect. Plasma osmolarity was highest in water-deprived animals, which also had high endogenous ADH.
questions.stem: What is the main effect of ADH on the collecting duct?

choices.C: It increases water reabsorption by increasing aquaporin-mediated permeability
choices.A: It decreases sodium reabsorption in all nephron segments
choices.B: It blocks all water movement into blood
choices.D: It directly filters proteins into urine

explanations.C: Correct. ADH increases water permeability in the collecting duct, allowing water reabsorption and producing lower-volume, more concentrated urine.
explanations.A: ADH is not described as globally decreasing sodium reabsorption.
explanations.B: The observed effect is increased water reabsorption, not blocked movement.
explanations.D: Healthy glomeruli generally prevent protein filtration.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Urine volume and osmolarity under baseline, ADH analog, aquaporin blockade, and water-deprivation conditions.

questions.stem: Why does aquaporin blockade blunt the ADH response?

choices.B: ADH requires water channels to increase collecting-duct water permeability
choices.A: ADH works only by digesting aquaporins
choices.C: Aquaporins pump sodium against its gradient
choices.D: The blocker increases endogenous ADH synthesis

explanations.B: Correct. If aquaporins are blocked, the collecting duct cannot increase water movement effectively even when ADH signaling is present.
explanations.A: ADH promotes aquaporin insertion/function rather than digestion.
explanations.C: Aquaporins are water channels, not sodium pumps.
explanations.D: The blocker affects water channels, not ADH synthesis.

questions.stem: Which condition produces the most concentrated urine in Figure 2?

explanations.A: Correct. Water deprivation has the highest plotted urine osmolarity, consistent with high endogenous ADH and water conservation.
explanations.C: Aquaporin blockade prevents most of the concentrating effect.
explanations.D: They are not the highest conditions.

questions.stem: Why does urine volume fall after ADH analog treatment?

choices.C: More water leaves the tubular fluid and returns to the blood
choices.D: Solute secretion into urine stops completely

explanations.C: Correct. ADH causes water reabsorption from the collecting duct, leaving less water to be excreted as urine.
explanations.A: The passage does not indicate complete cessation of filtration.
explanations.B: Bladder contraction is not the renal mechanism.
explanations.D: Urine concentration changes do not require all solute secretion to stop.

questions.stem: Which hormone system would most directly increase sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron?

explanations.B: Correct. Aldosterone increases sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron and collecting duct, often with potassium secretion.
explanations.D: Erythropoietin stimulates red blood cell production, not distal sodium reabsorption.
